summary: Broad-stack tests (also called end-to-end or full-stack tests) exercise most parts of a large application, in contrast to component tests that focus on isolated parts. The distinction between broad-stack and component tests exists on a continuum rather than as a clear boundary. Tests can be broad-stack even without UI manipulation if they exercise most of the software. Fowler recommends using test doubles for remote systems rather than calling them directly. While broad-stack tests excel at finding bugs in component interactions, they are slower and harder to maintain, leading to the Test Pyramid recommendation of using fewer of them.
key_points: The difference between broad-stack and component tests is a continuum, not a clear line
Tests can qualify as broad-stack even without UI manipulation (subcutaneous tests) if they exercise most of the system
Remote system calls should be replaced with test doubles and verified with contract tests to avoid slow, brittle tests
Broad-stack tests find interaction bugs that component tests miss, but are harder to maintain and slower to run
The Test Pyramid suggests using fewer broad-stack tests in favor of more component tests
money_quotes: The difference between a broad-stack test and a component test is a continuum rather than a clear line.
Many people, including myself, think that tests that call remote systems are unnecessarily slow and brittle.
It is usually better to use TestDoubles for these remote systems and check the doubles with ContractTests.

Broad-stack tests have the advantage of exercising the application with all its parts connected together and thus can find bugs in the interaction between components in the way that component tests cannot.
Broad-stack tests also tend to be harder to maintain and slower to run than component tests.
key_insight: Broad-stack tests are valuable for catching integration bugs but should be used sparingly per the Test Pyramid, with remote dependencies replaced by test doubles verified through contract tests.
